constraints: Write in first-person present tense ('I walk', 'I see', not 'I walked' or 'I saw').
It must explicitly be raining in every single scene.

turns.content: hey let's write a detective noir story together. the detective is a world-weary private eye in a 1940s city. start it off — he's just been hired by a mysterious woman

**The Client**

The rain hits my office window like it has a personal grievance, and I sit here in the dark with a cigarette I haven't lit and a bottle I'm trying not to open before noon.

She walks in at eleven forty-seven.

I know she's trouble the way you know a storm is coming — not from any single thing, but from the quality of the air. She wears a black dress and a hat with a veil and she moves like someone who has practiced not being noticed and given up halfway through. The rain has caught the hem of her coat and she doesn't seem to care.

"Mr. Dace?" she says.

"That's what the door says." I lean back in my chair. "What can I do for you, Miss—"

"Voss," she says. "Elena Voss." She sits down across from me without being asked, which I like. People who wait to be invited are usually lying about something. "My husband is missing."

"How long?"

"Four days." She opens her purse and sets an envelope on the desk between us. "The police say he's left me. That he has a history of this."

I look at the envelope. I don't touch it yet. "Does he?"

She looks at me through the veil and I can't read her eyes but I can read the set of her jaw. "No," she says. "He doesn't."

Outside the window the rain intensifies, rattling the pane in its frame. In this city, the rain always knows when to make a point.

"Tell me about your husband," I say, and I reach for the envelope.
great start! now write the scene where Dace goes to the husband's last known location — a jazz club. keep the rain and tense

**The Blue Canary**

The Blue Canary sits on the corner of Marsh and Eleventh like it's ashamed of the address. I turn my collar up against the rain and push through the door into a wall of cigarette smoke and a trumpet doing something mournful in the back.

It's two in the afternoon and the place has that specific quality of a nightclub trying to exist in daylight — wrong lighting, wrong faces, the barstools occupied by men who should be somewhere else. The rain hammers the skylight above the small stage where the trumpet player stands alone, his eyes closed, playing to nobody.

I take a stool at the bar. The bartender is a wide man with a scar on his chin that probably has a story I don't want to hear.

"You serve coffee?" I ask.

"This is a bar."

"Then I'll have whatever passes for scotch in this establishment." I set the photograph of Martin Voss on the bar between us. "You recognize this man?"

The bartender looks at it the way people look at things they've already decided not to know. "Can't say."

"Let me help your memory." I put two bills next to the photograph. "He came in four nights ago. Probably sat at this bar. Probably had more than one."

The bartender takes the bills without looking at them. The rain finds a gap in the skylight seal and drips onto the stage, a slow metronome that the trumpet player ignores.

"He was here," the bartender says. "Left around midnight. Wasn't alone when he left."

"Who was with him?"

The wide man looks at me. He looks at the door. He looks at the rain streaking down the front window. "Woman," he says. "Red dress. Didn't give a name."

I look at my untouched scotch. "They never do," I say.
write the scene where Dace interviews a witness — a street kid who saw something the night Martin disappeared

**The Kid**

I find the kid under the awning of a closed pawnshop on Eleventh, where the rain runs off the canvas in a solid curtain and pools on the sidewalk in a language only the gutter understands.

He's maybe twelve, thirteen — hard to tell with kids who've been living rough. He's got a newspaper folded over his head and a look in his eyes that's older than his face.

"You Scratch?" I ask.

He looks at me the way a cat looks at something it hasn't decided about yet. "Depends who's asking."

"Man named Dace. I'm not police."

"Everyone says that."

"Fair," I say. I hold out a coin. He doesn't take it, which is smart — takes the measure of a thing before you pick it up. "Manny at the newsstand says you work this corner most nights. Four nights ago, past midnight. A man came out of the Blue Canary. Maybe with a woman in red."

The kid is quiet for a moment. The rain doubles down behind me, turning the street into a mirror of moving light.

"I saw them," he says.

"Where did they go?"

"The woman had a car. A black one — no, dark green, the rain made it hard. The man, he wasn't — " The kid pauses. "He wasn't walking right. Like he was tired or like someone had put something in his drink."

My hand tightens around the coin. "Did anyone else get in the car?"

"A second man. He was waiting on the sidewalk. Bigger. He opened the back door and sort of — helped the first man in." The kid looks at the coin in my hand. "The big man looked up after. Right at me. I got behind the trash cans."

"Smart kid." I flip him the coin and another one. "Can you describe the big man?"

The kid catches both without looking. "Had a scar," he says, "here." He traces a line across his chin.

I think about the bartender at the Blue Canary. About the scar on his chin. About the way he took my money without looking at it.

The rain comes down. I stand in it for a moment, thinking.

"Stay out of sight," I tell the kid.

"Already planned on it," he says.
write the scene where Dace goes back to the Blue Canary to confront the bartender — it gets physical

**Second Visit**

The rain is coming sideways by the time I push back through the Blue Canary's door, and I'm in the kind of mood that the sideways rain produces.

The bartender sees me from across the room. He starts to reach for something below the bar, which tells me everything I need to know about the shape of the next few minutes.

I move fast. Fast enough that I'm around the bar before his hand finds whatever it's looking for, and I grab his wrist and put it flat against the wood.

"We need to revisit our conversation," I say.

He's bigger than me. He's been bigger than a lot of things in his life and learned to depend on it. He shoves with his whole body and I go back into the bar rail, which is not comfortable, but I keep his wrist.

"The man in the photograph," I say. "You helped put him in a car."

"I don't know what—"

I lean on his wrist. "A dark green car. At midnight. You were on the sidewalk."

He goes still. The trumpet player in the back has stopped. The rain hammers the skylight and drips onto the empty stage and the silence has a specific weight.

"I was told to," the bartender says finally.

"By who?"

"Man named Carver. He owns the building this place is in. He owns a lot of buildings." The bartender looks at his wrist and then at me. "He told me to make sure the man's drink was heavy and to whistle when he was ready."

"Where did the car go?"

"I don't know. I just — I just do what Carver says. Everybody on this block does."

I let his wrist go. He pulls it to his chest and rubs it. The rain finds the same gap in the skylight and drips onto the stage in the same place, patient and indifferent.

"Who is Carver?" I ask.

The bartender looks at the door. Looks at the rain on the window. Looks at me like I'm someone who doesn't understand how deep the water goes.

"He's the kind of man," the bartender says, "that you don't find. He finds you."
write the scene where Dace meets his police contact to get info on Carver

**Malone**

Detective Malone meets me under the railway bridge on Canal Street, which is where we always meet when the information is the kind that doesn't do well with witnesses. The rain can't reach us here — the bridge catches it — but I can hear it on the iron above us and see it turning the street beyond into a moving grey curtain.

"Carver," Malone says, like the name itself has a taste he doesn't like. He lights a cigarette and doesn't offer me one, which is our dynamic. "You sure you want to pull that thread?"

"Man's missing. His wife hired me."

"Men go missing in this city every day." He blows smoke toward the rain. "Most of the ones connected to Carver stay that way."

"That's reassuring. Who is he?"

Malone looks at the rain for a moment. "Victor Carver. Forty-eight years old. On paper he's a property developer — owns commercial buildings in the warehouse district, some residential on the south side. In practice he's the reason three judges don't sleep well and two aldermen don't make decisions without checking with him first."

"Is he connected to the department?"

"I didn't say that."

"You didn't not say it either."

He takes a long drag. "There are people in the department who find it convenient to look in different directions when Carver's name comes up. I am not one of those people, which is why I'm still a detective after fourteen years and not a lieutenant." He drops the cigarette. "What does a missing husband have to do with him?"

"Maybe nothing. Maybe Voss owed him something."

"If Voss owed Carver something, Voss is in a building somewhere in the warehouse district." Malone looks at me with the expression he uses when he's about to say something he wants to be able to deny later. "Pier Fourteen hasn't had a legitimate shipping lease in three years. It's Carver's and nobody asks why."

The rain picks up beyond the bridge. I watch it move.

"Thanks," I say.

"Don't thank me," Malone says. "Just don't tell me what you find." He turns up his collar and walks into the rain without another word.
write the scene where Dace goes to Pier 14 at night and finds something unexpected

**Pier Fourteen**

The rain at the waterfront is different from the rain in the city. In the city it bounces off concrete and ricochets off awnings and has somewhere to go. At the pier it just falls, straight and without interruption, into the black water below and onto the black wood above and onto me, standing at the entrance to a warehouse that is supposed to be empty.

The lock on the side door has been cut. Recently — the metal is bright where it was sheared, not yet clouded by the wet air. Someone else has been here tonight.

I push the door open and stand inside.

The warehouse is large and mostly dark. Crates along the walls, some stacked high, some broken open. A single work light hangs from a rafter at the far end, swaying slightly in a draft I can't find the source of. The rain hammers the roof in a continuous roar that makes the silence underneath it somehow louder.

I move toward the light.

Halfway there, I find Martin Voss.

He's alive. Tied to a chair, gagged, conscious — his eyes find me and go wide with something between relief and terror. There's blood on his collar but his colour is decent and his breathing when I pull the gag is ragged but present.

"How many?" I say quietly.

"Two men. They left — I don't know, an hour ago." His voice is wrecked. "There's something else here. In the crates. They were arguing about it when they left."

I pull my knife and start on the ropes. "What's in the crates?"

"I don't know. I heard them say it was enough to put Carver away for twenty years." He looks at me with the eyes of a man who has been sitting in the dark for four days. "Someone's been following him. Building a case. And he found out."

The work light sways. The rain roars on the roof. From somewhere outside — close, too close — I hear footsteps on the wet pier boards.

I get the last rope free and put my hand on Voss's arm. "Can you walk?"

"Yes."

"Then we're going to need to do that now," I say.
write the escape from the pier — Dace and Voss trying to get out while Carver's men return

**Out**

I push Voss toward the side door we came in through and the footsteps on the pier boards multiply — at least three men, maybe four, and they're not being quiet about it anymore, which means they already know something is wrong.

The rain outside the door is a solid wall. I go through it first and Voss comes right behind me, and we're immediately soaked, which is the least of our problems.

"This way," I say, keeping my voice at nothing.

We move along the building's side wall in the dark, away from the pier entrance where the footsteps are, toward the fence line at the back of the lot. The rain turns the ground under our feet into a slick of mud and gravel and I grab Voss's arm twice to keep him upright.

A light sweeps across the far end of the warehouse — a torch, someone checking the back before they go in the front. I pull Voss flat against the wall and we stand very still in the rain while the beam crosses the mud fifteen feet from us and keeps moving.

I count to five. We move.

The fence is chain-link and old and has a section near the back that's pulled away from the post — I found it on my approach, which is the kind of detail that matters now. I hold it open and Voss goes through and I follow and we're in the alley behind the pier, which smells of fish and standing water and the particular decay of a part of the city that nobody maintains.

"Where—" Voss starts.

"Not yet," I say.

We walk. The rain covers our sound and for once I'm grateful for it. Behind us I hear a shout — they've found the cut ropes — and then nothing, because the rain takes everything.

I have my car on Dock Road, three blocks east. We reach it without being followed, or if we're followed it's by someone better than the men I hear shouting at the pier.

Voss gets in. I get in. The rain hits the roof of the car in waves.

"My wife," he says.

"I know." I start the engine. "We'll get to her. First I need to know what you know about what's in those crates."

Voss looks at me in the dark of the car. The rain sheets down the windshield and makes the pier lights smear. "Everything," he says. "I know everything."
write the scene where Voss tells Dace what he knows — reveal the bigger conspiracy

**The Full Story**

I drive while Voss talks and the rain follows us through the warehouse district like it's been assigned.

"I work in the city assessor's office," he says. "Property records. Six months ago I started noticing irregularities — buildings being assessed at a quarter of their real value. Always the same cluster of properties. Always Carver's."

"He was avoiding property taxes."

"That would be the small version of it." Voss rubs his wrists where the ropes were. "I dug further. The buildings weren't just under-assessed. They were being used as transfer points — I found purchase records for properties that changed hands three, four times in a single week, always at inflated prices between shell companies. The kind of transactions that take dirty money and make it look like real estate profit."

I turn the car onto a quieter street. The rain is steady here, drumming the roof with metronomic patience. "He was laundering money through his own property portfolio."

"Through the city's property records. With the help of two assessors and at least one official in the city clerk's office." Voss looks at his hands. "When I realized what I was seeing, I was going to go to the DA. But I wanted evidence first. Something physical. So I followed the paper trail to the pier."

"The crates."

"Financial documents. Ledgers. The real ones — the ones that show the actual transactions, not the cleaned versions they file with the city. Someone else had already found them. Someone who'd been building a case longer than I had." He pauses. "There were photographs in the crates too. Of Carver meeting with the two aldermen. One of them is on the city council's budget committee."

The rain intensifies. The wipers can barely keep up. I think about what Malone told me — the judges who don't sleep well, the aldermen who check with Carver before deciding anything.

"Who else knows what you found?" I ask.

Voss is quiet for a moment. "My wife doesn't know any of this. I kept her out of it."

"Not her. Who else in the assessor's office?"

He looks at me. The rain makes the streetlights bloom and smear on the windshield between us.

"No one," he says. "Unless—" He stops. "Unless someone saw me looking."
write the scene where Dace reunites Voss with his wife Elena — but something feels off about her reaction

**Reunion**

I bring Voss to the hotel on Morrison where I've been keeping Elena since this morning, when it became clear that her house on Aldgate was a place Carver's people knew about. The rain hasn't let up and the hotel awning does almost nothing, so we're both soaked when we reach the second floor and I knock on the door marked 214.

Elena opens it in four seconds, which means she's been near the door.

She looks at her husband. She doesn't move for a moment — just looks at him, at the blood on his collar, at the four days written across his face — and then she steps forward and puts her hand on his cheek, and that's when I notice the thing that isn't right.

She isn't surprised.

Not just controlled — she genuinely doesn't have the expression of someone who has been told the worst and now sees the opposite. There's relief there, yes. But not the flooding kind. The relief of someone whose plan is proceeding rather than the relief of someone who was afraid it might not.

I don't say anything. I stand in the doorway with rain dripping from my coat and I watch them, and Voss holds her and closes his eyes and doesn't see her face.

"I knew you'd find him," she says to me, over his shoulder. Her eyes are steady. Dry. "You came very highly recommended."

"Who recommended me?" I ask. I know the answer she gave me in my office. I want to hear if she changes it.

"A friend," she says. "I told you."

I nod slowly. "Right," I say. "You did."

I step back into the hallway. The rain hits the window at the end of the corridor and makes the light shift. I stand there for a moment, listening to the murmur of husband and wife through the closed door, and I think about a woman who came to my office at eleven forty-seven in the morning and sat down without being asked.

People who wait to be invited are usually lying about something.

People who don't wait are too.
write the scene where Dace investigates Elena's background and finds something unexpected

**The Other File**

I go back to my office in the rain and pull everything I can find on Elena Voss, née Marsh, which is publicly not much and privately more than she'd like.

The city directory has her at the Aldgate address for three years, married to Martin Voss, no occupation listed. The records before that are thinner. A lease on an apartment in the Strand district under Elena Marsh, two years, ending when she married. Before that — a gap. A name that doesn't appear in the usual places for two years.

I call in a favour from a woman named Bette who works in the county records office and owes me since the unpleasantness with her brother-in-law eighteen months ago.

Bette calls back in twenty minutes, while the rain goes sideways again and I stand at my window watching it push pedestrians into doorways.

"Elena Marsh," Bette says. "Or that's the name on the records. There's a prior name in the marriage index — a first marriage, annulled in 1938. Name on the annulment record for the other party is Victor Carver."

I stand very still at my window. The rain keeps moving.

"She was married to Carver," I say.

"Briefly. Six months. Annulled, not divorced — the grounds listed are administrative, which usually means someone with influence wanted it quiet."

I think about Elena sitting across from me in my office. The way she said Carver's name when I mentioned it this morning — or rather, the way she didn't say it, because I realize now that I said it and she gave no reaction at all. No flash of recognition. No flinching.

No reaction from a woman who used to share a name with the man.

"Thank you, Bette," I say.

"You didn't get this from me," she says, and hangs up.

I put the phone down and look at my reflection in the rain-streaked window. A woman who was married to Carver. Who knew about the pier because she'd been told. Who hired a private detective not to find her husband, but to extract him once she was ready.

The question I turn over in the rain-grey light is simple and not simple at all: is Elena Voss trying to destroy Victor Carver, or is she still working for him?
write the confrontation scene — Dace goes back to Elena and lays it all out

**The Real Story**

The rain is still going when I knock on 214 again, and when Elena opens the door she sees my face and steps back to let me in without a word, which tells me she's been expecting this moment.

Voss is asleep on the bed behind her. Good. Some of what I'm about to say he doesn't need to hear from this angle.

"Elena Marsh," I say. "Previously Elena Carver."

She sits down in the chair by the window. The rain streams down the glass behind her, making patterns that shift and dissolve. She doesn't deny it.

"What do you want to know?" she says.

"Everything you left out of the version you told me in my office."

She looks at her hands for a moment. "I was nineteen when I met Victor. He was charming and then he wasn't and by the time I understood the difference I'd been in his orbit long enough to know things I couldn't unknow." She pauses. "He ended the marriage because I became inconvenient. He had the annulment arranged in a week. When you're Victor Carver that kind of thing takes a week."

"And Martin?"

"I met Martin four years later. He doesn't know about Victor. I didn't think he needed to." She looks up at me. "When Martin started bringing home documents — started talking about irregularities in the records — I recognized what he'd found. Because I'd seen those kinds of irregularities before. From the inside."

"So you knew where this was going."

"I knew Victor would find out. I knew Martin would disappear." She holds my gaze. "I hired you because I needed someone to get him out before Victor decided the situation required a permanent solution."

The rain intensifies. The window rattles once.

"Why not go to the police?" I ask.

"Victor has police," she says. "I needed someone who was just one person. One person without a department, without a chain of command, without anyone Carver could reach."

I look at her for a long time. "You used me."

"Yes," she says. "Are you telling me you wouldn't have taken the case if I'd told you all of this in your office?"

I think about the envelope on my desk. About the rain on my window. About a cigarette I hadn't lit.

"No," I say. "I'd have taken it. But I'd have charged you more."
write the scene where Dace decides what to do with the evidence in the crates — he has to make a choice

**The Weight**

I drive back to the pier alone.

The rain hasn't stopped and the pier is empty now — the men Carver sent have gone, probably to report to someone about what they found and didn't find and what their next move looks like. I have maybe an hour before that conversation produces an answer.

I stand inside the warehouse under the swaying work light and look at the crates.

I could take the ledgers to the DA. The DA is a man named Holt who I've met twice and who has the kind of righteous confidence that's either admirable or dangerous depending on how high up the corruption goes. If Carver's reach extends to Holt's office — and after tonight I don't assume it doesn't — then the ledgers disappear and the men who handled them disappear shortly after.

I could take them to Malone. Malone is clean, I believe that, but Malone is one man in a department where loyalty runs sideways in ways even Malone can't fully map.

I could take them to the newspapers. There's a reporter named Flynn at the Courier who has been writing around the edges of Carver's operation for two years, getting close and then getting warned and then writing something else instead. The newspapers have their own pressures. Their own owners. Their own arrangements.

The rain drums on the roof. The work light sways. I'm standing in a wet warehouse at two in the morning holding the question of who in this city can be trusted to take something true and let it remain true.

The answer I arrive at is uncomfortable: nobody alone.

I start loading crates into my car. I'm going to need Malone, and Flynn, and probably Holt, all at the same time, with copies in multiple hands before any one of them knows the others have it. The thing about corruption is that it operates in the dark by arrangement. You fight it with simultaneous light.

It takes four trips in the rain to load everything.

By the third trip I've stopped feeling the wet. By the fourth I'm just thinking about who to call first and in what order and how fast this city can move when it wants to bury something.

Faster, usually, than one man in a wet coat.

But I've been underestimated before.
write the scene where Carver himself reaches out to Dace — wants to make a deal

**Carver**

The call comes to my office at seven in the morning, while the rain falls straight and grey through air that hasn't warmed up since yesterday.

"Mr. Dace." The voice is smooth. The kind of smooth that's been worked on, the way a con man's hands are worked on — not natural, but practiced into something that passes. "My name is Carver. I imagine you know why I'm calling."

"I have a guess."

"I'd like to meet. Today. In a location of your choosing, which I offer as a gesture of good faith."

I look at the rain on my window and think about gestures of good faith from men like Victor Carver. "What kind of meeting?"

"The kind where I make you an offer and you consider it. Nothing more complicated than that." A pause. "I'm going to make it regardless of your current disposition toward me. The question is whether you'd like to hear it in person or by letter."

I name a diner on Clement Street — public, windows facing the street, a waitress named Dot who I've been eating breakfast at for six years and who notices everything. "Noon," I say.

"I'll be there."

He is.

Carver is a compact man in a grey suit that costs more than my monthly rent. He sits across from me in the booth with his hands folded on the table and the rain tracing lines down the window beside us. He looks like a banker and has the eyes of something older and colder than banking.

"The documents," he says.

"What about them?"

"I'll pay twice whatever Elena paid you. Plus an amount to cover your sustained services in future matters, should you find that arrangement agreeable." He waits. "The documents, Mr. Dace, and your silence on the matter of Martin Voss. He goes home, his wife goes home, nothing happened."

I look at him across the table. The rain makes the window beside us a grey moving wall. Dot refills my coffee without being asked, glances at Carver once, and moves away.

"And the men in the assessor's office," I say. "The aldermen. The pier."

"Ancient history."

"This morning's ancient history."

He watches me with those cold eyes. "Everyone has a number, Mr. Dace."

"Mine," I say, "is not in a category you can count to." I put my cup down. "But I appreciate the coffee conversation. Very civilized."

Carver looks at me for a long moment. The rain comes down. He picks up his hat from the seat beside him.

"I find out you've moved against me," he says, very quietly, "and civilized stops being the relevant word."

"I'll make a note of that," I say.
write the scene where Dace coordinates with Malone and Flynn to release the evidence — building tension

**Simultaneous**

The logistics of simultaneous disclosure take most of the afternoon and happen in three separate locations, because the three men involved can't be seen together and because I spend most of the afternoon running between them in the rain, which has decided today is a day for the patient, committed variety rather than the dramatic.

Malone gets the ledgers first. We meet at the railway bridge again — it's becoming our office — and I lay out what I have while the rain falls straight and soft beyond the bridge's shadow.

"If I take these to the Captain," Malone says, turning a page, "and the Captain is one of Carver's people—"

"You don't take them to the Captain yet. You hold them. You don't file them, you don't log them, you keep them at home under your bed or wherever honest detectives keep things they're not ready to declare. You wait for my signal."

He looks at me steadily. "When's the signal?"

"When Flynn publishes."

Flynn is at the Courier, in a back office that smells of ink and old coffee and the kind of smoke that comes from a man who thinks better with a cigarette. The rain pings on the metal roof above us while I walk him through the photographs and the shell company documents.

"This is enough," he says, and I can hear the hunger in it that reporters get when a story they've been circling for years suddenly sits down in front of them. "This is more than enough."

"You run nothing until I say. And when I say, you run everything — front page, all of it, simultaneously with Malone filing his report."

Flynn looks at me. "How simultaneous?"

"Same hour. You're on the phone with Malone while your editor is laying the front page."

He chews his lip. The rain doubles on the roof. "And the DA?"

"I go to Holt last. With copies I've kept. After Malone files and after your paper hits the stands, Holt can't bury it — it's already public record and already in the official file simultaneously." I look at him. "The thing about Carver is that he works by controlling single points. Give him a single point to control and he controls it. Give him three at once and the math changes."

Flynn nods slowly, looking at the photographs. The rain comes down on the roof. On the street outside the window the city moves in its grey wet afternoon, not knowing yet what's about to change.

"What's your timeline?" he asks.

"Forty-eight hours," I say. "Maybe less, depending on what Carver does next."

And forty-eight hours turns out to be optimistic.
write the scene where Carver makes his move — Dace gets jumped

**Ambush**

It happens the same night, in the alley behind my office building, which means either I'm being watched or Carver is faster than I gave him credit for, and neither option is particularly flattering.

The rain is the heavy kind that turns alleys into rivers. I hear nothing above it, which is the point — they time their movement to the sound of rain on the metal fire escape above us, and the first one hits me from the left before I even have a clear look at the alley.

I go into the brick wall shoulder-first and spin and the second one catches me across the jaw, which produces the specific bright interior light that precedes the decision about whether to stay upright. I stay upright. I've made worse decisions.

There are three of them. Big men in dark coats with the professional economy of movement that comes from doing this for a living. The rain streams off them and off me and off the fire escape above us in a continuous curtain.

I get one good shot in — the centre one, the largest — before the third gets behind me and puts an arm across my throat. I pull forward and stomp and he loosens enough. I get my elbow into the space his loosening creates and he goes back into a stack of crates.

The first one comes back. He has something in his hand.

The sound when it connects with the side of my head is the specific sound of being finished for the evening.

I'm on the wet ground in the alley and the rain is coming straight down onto my face and I'm looking at the black rectangle of sky between the buildings. I can hear them going through my coat pockets. I can hear rain on brick. I can hear nothing useful.

"Tell Carver," one of them says, very near my ear, "that the next conversation goes further."

Then they're gone and the alley is just me and the rain and the slow understanding that I am missing the copies I was carrying.

Carver is fast. I'll give him that.

I lie in the rain for a moment, doing an inventory of what still works. It's longer than it should be and shorter than it might have been.

Then I get up, because the alternative is worse.
write the scene where Dace, injured, has to regroup — he goes to an unexpected person for help

**Unexpected Help**

I walk six blocks in the rain with what is probably a cracked rib and certainly a split lip and the specific headache that precedes a doctor's advice you're not going to follow.

I end up at Elena Voss's hotel room door, which is not where I planned to end up, but planning has taken some damage this evening along with everything else.

She opens the door and takes one look at me and steps back. She doesn't say anything. The rain is audible on the corridor window behind me.

"They took the copies I was carrying," I say.

"I know," she says. "Sit down."

Martin is awake, sitting at the small table with a cup of something, and he gets up when he sees me and goes to the bathroom and comes back with a damp cloth, which I press to my lip without comment.

"There are other copies," Elena says. She's standing at the window with the rain making slow lines behind her. "I should have told you earlier. Before you made your plan."

I look at her over the cloth. "Where?"

"Victor always kept documents that could be used against him in two places. Physical security — multiple copies, different locations, so that the loss of one didn't mean the loss of everything." She pauses. "He taught me that. Early on, when I was still someone he trusted with how he thought." Her mouth does something complicated. "There's a safety deposit box at the Meridian Bank on Fourth. It's in a name I can access. I've had a key since 1938 and he doesn't know I kept it because he never thought I was worth worrying about."

I'm quiet for a moment. The rain comes down on the window. Martin refills his cup and brings one to me without asking.

"He kept his own insurance," I say.

"Victor plans for everything except the people he underestimates." She looks at me. "Tomorrow morning. The bank opens at nine."

"Carver will be watching the bank."

"Not the side entrance. There's a service corridor connected to the adjoining building." She pauses. "I spent six months married to him and I spent the six years after that learning everything I hadn't learned while I was."

I press the cloth to my lip and look at the rain on the window and think about a woman I have misjudged in two separate directions in three days.

"Tell me about the side entrance," I say.
write the bank scene — Dace and Elena retrieve the second set of documents

**The Meridian**

The rain in the morning has a different quality than the rain at night — softer, steadier, the kind that settles into your coat before you realise it's doing it. I come through the service entrance of the Calloway Building at eight fifty-five, cross the connecting corridor to the Meridian Bank's back office, and meet Elena at the staff door where she's standing with a key in her hand and the focused calm of someone doing a thing they've been planning for a long time.

The clerk in the vault access room is a young man who doesn't ask questions, which either means he doesn't know anything or he knows exactly where his discretion should be and has made peace with it. Elena gives the account name — a name I don't recognise — and the clerk takes the key and opens the access door without comment.

The box is larger than I expect.

We take it to a private room and close the door and I open it.

Inside: three folders, a roll of film, and a ledger that is different from the ones at the pier — older, thicker, the entries in Carver's own hand. I turn pages in the private room while the rain taps at the high window above us and Elena stands with her arms folded and watches.

"He kept this himself," I say.

"The one he'd never let anyone else touch." She looks at the ledger. "Those are the payments. To the judges, the aldermen, the department contacts. In his handwriting. With dates going back eleven years."

Eleven years. The scope of it sits in the room for a moment, heavier than the rain.

"The film?" I ask.

"I don't know. He put it there in '41. I only accessed the box once, to see what he was keeping. I never touched anything."

I take everything. The ledger, the folders, the film. I put it into the bag I've brought and I zip it closed and I look at Elena across the small table.

"This is enough," I say. "This is more than enough."

She looks at the closed bag. Something in her face shifts — not quite relief, not quite grief. The expression of someone who has been carrying something for a long time and can feel the weight begin to change.

"Then let's finish it," she says, and we go back out through the corridor into the rain.
write the climax — Dace coordinates the release and Carver makes one final desperate move

**The Hour**

I spend the morning making calls from a payphone on the corner of Clement and Ninth, in the rain, which is the appropriate place for a man making calls he can't make from his own office.

Malone gets the ledger and the folders. I go through every page with him, standing in a doorway on Arch Street while the rain comes off the eave above us in a curtain. He makes notes in his small book and his face does the thing it does when he finds something that confirms what he's already suspected: nothing visible, just a stillness.

"Eleven years," he says.

"The payments go back eleven. The laundering goes back seven."

"I'll need to go to the federal contacts. The department's too compromised for this to stay local."

"That's your call," I say. "Make it at noon. Not before."

Flynn gets the photographs and the shell company documents — the pier material, plus the film from the safety deposit box, which turns out to be meeting photographs that are deeply clarifying about the relationship between Carver and two men who currently serve on the city's oversight board. Flynn's hands are shaking slightly when he looks at them. Good. Shaking hands move faster.

"Noon," I tell him.

"The editor is already—"

"Noon."

At eleven forty I go to the DA's office with copies I've kept and Holt, to his credit, listens without interrupting. The rain hits the windows of his office and outside I can see the city going about its morning under the grey sky, not yet knowing.

At eleven fifty-five my phone at the office rings. I'm back there by then, standing at the window in my wet coat.

"You think you've been clever," Carver says.

"I think I've been thorough," I say.

"There's a woman," he says. "And her husband. The hotel on Morrison."

My hand tightens on the phone. I look at the rain on the window. "If you touch either of them—"

"I haven't yet." A pause. Calculated. "There's still time to stop this."

"It's eleven fifty-seven," I say.

The clock in my office moves.

"And at noon?" Carver says.

"At noon," I say, "it stops mattering what you do."

I hear him breathe. I hear the rain. I hear the city existing in its last three minutes of not knowing.

At noon, Malone files. Flynn's paper hits the wire. Holt issues a statement.

The phone is still in my hand but the line is dead. Carver has hung up, which is the only thing he has left to do.

I stand at the window and watch the rain and I breathe, carefully, around the rib that's probably cracked, and I think: it's not over. It'll be weeks of lawyers and hearings and men trying to find edges in the evidence. But the simultaneous light is on. And this city, for all its arrangements and accommodations, can't put that back in the dark.

Not today.
write the falling action — Dace wraps up with the Voss family. a quiet scene

**Settlement**

The afternoon is the kind that comes after decisions — quieter, the rain having settled into a thin persistent drizzle that isn't dramatic enough to avoid but isn't heavy enough to warrant the umbrella. The kind of rain that just is.

I find the Vosses in their hotel room, Martin sitting at the small table and Elena by the window where the drizzle tacks and drips. They've been waiting to know what the morning means.

I tell them.

Martin looks at the table for a long time. "The men in my office. The ones who were helping Carver with the assessments."

"They're on the list of names Malone is working through," I say. "They'll have a harder time than Carver, honestly. They'll want to trade what they know. It'll be ugly and slow and legal."

"And Victor?" Elena asks.

"Carver's gone to ground. Probably been planning a version of this since he got the call this morning." I look at her. "He's not the kind of man who doesn't have an exit."

"No," she says. "He isn't."

The drizzle moves on the window behind her. Martin reaches across the table and puts his hand over hers and she lets him, which is the first natural thing I've seen between them in three days.

"You can go home," I say. "The house on Aldgate is probably safe now. Carver's people are going to be very busy not being caught."

"Will he come back?" Martin asks.

I think about the question honestly. "Probably not to you specifically. He's got larger problems." I stand up. "But you're going to want to talk to a lawyer about the assessor's records. What you found, how you found it, your cooperation with the investigation. Get that on the record cleanly and early."

Elena walks me to the door. She puts an envelope in my hand — the rest of the payment, I assume, plus the additional we negotiated in the hotel room after I laid out what I knew about her and Carver.

"I'm sorry I wasn't more honest with you," she says.

I look at her. The drizzle makes the corridor window a soft grey behind her. "You were honest enough," I say. "You hired me for the job you hired me for and I did it. How you got there is your business."

She nods, once. It's not quite gratitude and not quite anything else.

I take the stairs down and push through the hotel door into the drizzle, and the afternoon city closes around me.
write the final scene — Dace back in his office, reflecting. classic noir ending

**The Office, Evening**

The rain comes back in the evening the way it always does — with renewed commitment, like it's been thinking about what it wants to say all afternoon and has finally decided.

I sit in my office in the dark with the lamp off and the window open an inch, enough to hear it but not enough to let it in. My split lip has closed. My rib hurts at a predictable interval. My desk has the envelope from Elena on it and the photograph of Martin Voss that she left behind in her haste, which I should probably mail to the hotel but probably won't.

I light the cigarette I didn't light this morning.

The newspapers have Carver's name on the front page. The radio is running it every half hour. The city is going through the particular experience of learning that the arrangements it suspected were as extensive as the most cynical of them believed, and reacting with the specific combination of shock and recognition that people reserve for things they half-knew.

Malone calls at eight. The federal contacts have taken over the case file. Three men from the assessor's office are cooperating. Two aldermen have retained lawyers, which is how you know the lawyers will be working late.

Carver himself is not yet in custody. Malone says this without apparent surprise. I receive it the same way. Men like Carver don't stay for the reckoning — they built the exits before they built the operation, which is a kind of planning I reluctantly respect.

I think about Elena by the window of her hotel room. About a woman who spent six months inside a mechanism and six years building the understanding to take it apart. About the way she said he never thought I was worth worrying about, with a flatness that was the very last stage of grief.

I think about the kid under the awning on Eleventh — Scratch — and the coin I flipped him and the scar he traced across his chin. He won't testify to anything. He knows better. But he saw what he saw and told me what he told me, and sometimes the truth gets carried by the people least expected to carry it.

I think about Martin Voss sitting in the dark of a warehouse for four days, holding what he knew and staying alive.

Outside, the rain intensifies again, a brief assault that turns the window into a waterfall before settling back to its evening rhythm. The city absorbs it, the way it absorbs everything — not gracefully, not without damage, but continuously.

This city is going to wake up tomorrow still recognisably itself. Some of the men who ran things yesterday will run different things next week. Some of the arrangements will come apart and new ones will be assembled from the wreckage. The rain will continue, because it always continues.

But somewhere in the Meridian Bank's records — and in Malone's files, and in Flynn's paper, and in the DA's case — is an eleven-year ledger in a man's own handwriting. And that doesn't go away.

I finish the cigarette.

I close the window against the rain.

It keeps falling anyway.
that ending was perfect. can you write a new case for Dace? just the opening — a new client, a different kind of trouble

**Case Two: The Painter**

The rain has been continuous for six days, which in this city is either a record or just a Tuesday, and my office smells of damp wool and old coffee and the particular regret of a man who has been working too many consecutive hours.

The new client knocks at three in the afternoon.

He's not the type I usually get — a young man, maybe twenty-five, with paint-stained hands and a good coat and the expression of someone who hasn't slept in longer than he'd like to admit. He sits across from my desk and the rain makes its usual commentary on the window beside us.

"My name is Laszlo Kren," he says. "My teacher is dead."

"I'm sorry to hear it," I say. "That sounds like a matter for the police."

"The police say it was an accident," he says. "A fall in his studio. The rain had made the skylight glass slippery and he was up on the scaffold and—" He stops. His hands are flat on his knees. "Emil Varga didn't fall. He had been on that scaffold every day for eleven years. He knew every slippery inch of it."

I look at the paint on his hands. Cobalt and something darker. "What do the police say about that?"

"They say grief makes people see things that aren't there." His jaw tightens. "Two days before Emil died, someone came to the studio. I didn't see them — I was in the back room. But I heard Emil say: you can't have it, and then a long silence, and then the door."

"And after that?"

"He was different. Quiet. He packed up three canvases and told me he was sending them to a collector in the city for safekeeping." Kren looks at me. "He's never done that before. He always kept his work close. He said once that paintings are hostages to the people who want to own them."

The rain comes down. I reach for my pad.

"Tell me about Emil Varga," I say.
write the scene where Dace investigates the painter's studio — what does he find?

**The Studio**

The studio is on the fourth floor of a building on Meridian Lane that was already old when the century was new, and the rain gets into the stairwell from a window that's been broken at the second floor landing and never repaired, so I'm wet before I even reach the studio door.

Kren has a key. The police seal was broken yesterday when the landlord came to take inventory — it's that kind of city, that kind of landlord — so we go in without ceremony.

The studio is high and wide and lit from above through a long skylight that is, as advertised, still standing open on one section. The rain drops through it in a thin steady line onto a tarp that Kren has laid there at some point. The rest of the room is dry.

I look at the scaffold first.

It's a simple structure — four vertical poles, a platform, a rail. The platform is at the height of the upper third of the largest canvas on the north wall, which is a painting I spend a moment looking at. It's large and dark and has the quality of something that took a long time and a specific kind of obsession. The skylight above the scaffold is closed except for the open section at the far end, which is ten feet from where the platform sits.

"The police said he fell from here," Kren says, beside me.

I look at the distance between the platform and the open skylight section. I look at the floor below — a wooden floor, pale and clean except for old paint stains. I look at the rail on the scaffold, which comes to hip height on a standing man.

"He would have had to go over the rail," I say. "Or through it."

Kren nods. "The rail is intact."

I examine the platform boards. There are marks there — not the slide marks of a fall, but two parallel lines in the paint dust on the boards, the kind that come from something heavy being dragged.

"He was moved," I say. "Someone moved him onto the floor after."

Kren says nothing, because there's nothing to say to that.

I move to the south wall, where the empty hooks tell me where the three missing canvases were. I look at the hooks — no scrapes, no damage, removed carefully, by someone who knew what they were doing.

"The collector Emil sent the paintings to," I say. "You have a name?"

"Roth," Kren says. "A man named Solomon Roth. He lives in the Belvedere apartments on Sixth."

The rain comes through the open skylight section and hits the tarp with a soft continuous sound. I look at the scaffold and the empty hooks and the dark ambitious painting on the north wall, and I think about a man who said paintings are hostages to the people who want to own them.

"Let's go see Mr. Roth," I say.
write the scene where Dace meets Solomon Roth — mysterious and not what he expected

**Roth**

The Belvedere is the kind of building that was respectable before the war and has maintained the posture of respectability without fully committing to the practice. The lobby has marble floors that haven't been polished in six months and a doorman who looks at my coat, which is still wet from the walk through the rain on Sixth Street, and decides I'm tolerable but not impressive.

Roth is on the seventh floor. He answers the door himself, which I don't expect from a man in his apparent bracket.

He's old — seventy, seventy-five — with white hair and the kind of stillness that belongs to people who have spent most of their life paying attention to things other people walk past. He looks at me for a long moment and then at Kren, who is behind me in the hallway, and then he opens the door wider.

"I wondered when someone would come," he says.

His apartment is full of art. Not the decorative kind — the kind that came from somewhere specific and means something specific and is not arranged but accumulated, the way a man's beliefs accumulate, in overlapping proximity. The rain on the large windows gives the paintings a grey ambient light that I find unexpectedly appropriate.

"The three canvases Emil sent," I say, settling into the chair he indicates. "Where are they?"

"Safe," he says. "Which is what Emil wanted. He called me four days ago. He told me someone had come to the studio asking about a particular work — the large one on the north wall. The commission piece."

"Asking to buy it?"

"Asking to have it." Roth's eyes are sharp in the old face. "There is a difference. The man who came did not have the quality of a buyer. Emil said he had the quality of a retrieval."

I feel the rain on the window. I feel the shape of something coming into clarity. "What's in the commission piece?"

Roth looks at me for a moment. Then he stands, with the deliberate movement of old bones, and goes to a cabinet on the far wall. He comes back with a folder and sets it on the table between us.

Inside the folder are documents. Not financial records. Historical ones — certificates, letters in several languages, a chain of provenance for an artwork of significant value that passed through several hands in Europe during the late 1930s and arrived in this city in 1941.

"Emil didn't paint the commission piece," Roth says. "He was paid to authenticate it as his own."

The rain comes down.

"By whom?" I ask.

Roth looks at the folder. "By the man who brought it here from a collection in Prague. A collection that was not his to bring."
write the scene where the deeper truth about the painting is revealed — it's looted art from the war

**Provenance**

Roth takes the three canvases from the back room himself, carrying them one at a time with the care of a man who handles irreplaceable things and knows it. The rain doubles on the windows as he brings the third one out and leans all three against the sitting room wall.

I look at them. They're not the commission piece — they're Emil Varga's own work, unmistakably, the same hand as the dark ambitious painting on the studio's north wall but smaller, earlier. Kren makes a small sound when he sees them, the involuntary sound of recognition.

"Emil sent his own work," Roth says, "because he couldn't send the commission piece without the man knowing. He sent what he loved instead." He pauses. "He knew what was coming."

I look at the provenance documents in the folder. The chain of ownership for the commission piece — which is not, I understand now, an Emil Varga at all. It's an older work, a significant one, attributed to a Viennese painter whose name appears in the documents in a way that carries weight I can feel even without knowing the full context.

"The collection in Prague," I say. "Whose was it?"

Roth's hands are folded in his lap. The rain on the window makes the grey light shift. "A family named Kleiner. Collectors. The collection was extensive — sixty years of acquisition, a significant library, works of considerable value." He looks at the folder. "They did not leave Prague. They left behind everything they couldn't carry, and some of what they left behind was carried by other people who arrived after them."

"And the commission piece was in that collection."

"The man who brought it here in '41 had papers. They looked correct because he had access to the kind of people who make papers look correct. He wanted it legitimized — wanted it to be an Emil Varga so that the legitimate version of the provenance started here, in this city, clean." Roth meets my eyes. "Emil told me all of this. Two years ago. He was ashamed. He had been afraid, and then he had been ashamed, and he had been living with both."

"Who is the man?" I ask.

Roth tells me the name.

I recognise it. I think most people in this city would recognise it. It's on a building on the corner of Fifth and the river, where it has been for twenty years, in letters that are cleaned twice a year and lit at night.

Kren, beside me, has gone very still.

The rain falls on Roth's windows and on the river building's letters equally, without distinction or preference, which is the rain's one democratic quality.

"Kleiner," I say. "Is there family left?"

"There is," Roth says quietly. "A daughter. She lives in Brooklyn now. She has been looking for what her family left behind for twelve years." He looks at the three canvases against his wall. "Emil knew. He wrote her a letter. He was going to send it."

"Did he?"

"He sent it the day before he died," Roth says.
write the scene where Dace identifies the man responsible — a respected figure in the city — and decides what to do

**The Name**

The rain follows me back to my office the way it follows everything in this city — steadily, without apology, as though it was there first and the rest of us are the newcomers.

I sit at my desk and look at the name Roth gave me written on my notepad and think about what it means to have it.

Harold Fenn. Sixty-three years old, civic philanthropist, chairman of the arts endowment, donor to three city hospitals and the public library's new east wing. His name is on a building on Fifth and the river. His photograph appears in the society pages beside mayors and visiting dignitaries. His collection — which he opens to public viewing twice a year — is considered one of the finest in the city.

I think about Emil Varga on that scaffold. About the parallel drag marks in the paint dust on the platform boards. About a man who said you can't have it and then died in a way the police called an accident because the police in this city have a variety of reasons to call things accidents when convenient.

I could go to the police. Malone is clean, but this isn't a financial corruption case. This is a murder in which the physical evidence is suggestive but not conclusive. The drag marks. The position of the body. An old man's testimony about what a dead painter told him. It builds a picture. It doesn't, in the way a courtroom requires, close the frame.

I could go to the newspapers. Flynn is already grateful and probably ready. But an accusation against Harold Fenn without sufficient evidence doesn't become justice. It becomes a lawsuit and a retraction and an end to Flynn's career.

The rain beats on my window. I think about a letter Emil sent to a woman in Brooklyn the day before he died.

I pick up the phone and I call information and I get a number in Brooklyn and I dial it.

It rings four times.

"Hello?" A woman's voice. Careful. The voice of someone who has learned to be careful about unexpected calls.

"My name is Dace," I say. "I'm a private detective. I'm calling about a letter you may have recently received from a man named Emil Varga."

A silence. The rain comes down.

"I received it yesterday," she says.

"Then you know what your family lost," I say. "And I may know what happened to the man who was trying to return it. Are you willing to talk?"

Another silence. Longer.

"Yes," she says. "I'm willing."

I look at Harold Fenn's name on my notepad. Men like Fenn operate on the assumption that their position makes them safe. That the gap between what they did and what can be proven is where they live.

But there is a letter. And the woman who wrote it kept a twelve-year record of her search. And there is Roth, who is old and has no remaining reason to be afraid of anything. And there are the drag marks, which a proper forensic examination of the studio — one conducted with cause rather than a cursory police walk-through — might yet read clearly.

And there is me, sitting in the rain with a name on a notepad, adding things up.

"I'll come to you," I tell the woman. "Tomorrow. We have a lot to talk about."

I hang up and sit in the dark of my office and the rain comes down on the city with the patient consistency of something that doesn't distinguish between a philanthropist's well-lit building and a wet man's single window.

That's the thing about rain. It doesn't know whose side it's on.

That's my job.
write a scene where Dace travels to Brooklyn and meets the woman — Ruth Kleiner

**Ruth**

The rain crosses the bridge with me, which I suppose I should stop being surprised by.

Brooklyn in the morning has a different quality from the city across the water — quieter, lower, the streets here proportioned for lives rather than commerce. The address Ruth Kleiner gave me is a narrow house on a street of narrow houses, each with a small garden that is currently a small lake.

She answers before I finish knocking.

Ruth Kleiner is perhaps forty-five, with grey threading through dark hair and the particular quality of composure that belongs to people who have had to be composed about very large things for a very long time. She looks at me the way Roth looked at me — like someone who has been expecting a version of this and is measuring whether I'm the right version.

"Come in," she says. "I made coffee."

The front room is small and warm and has books in two languages and a photograph on the wall that stops me — a family, formally arranged, in a room full of paintings. The paintings are visible in the background, an accumulation of them, wall to wall.

"Prague," she says, behind me. "1937."

I look at the photograph. I can identify three of the paintings from Roth's documents.

We sit down with the coffee and the rain on her window and she puts Emil's letter on the table between us, already well-read, the folds soft.

"He apologized," she says. "For twenty years of silence. For his fear. He was very specific about that — he said cowardice and fear are not the same thing but that the distinction hadn't helped anyone."

"He named Fenn?"

"He named him. He described how it was arranged. The papers, the authentication, the gallery showing that established the painting in this city as his work." She looks at the letter. "He said the painting was called The Interior, Afternoon. My father bought it in Vienna in 1931. I know it from photographs."

I drink the coffee. The rain comes down on her window and on the small drowned garden outside.

"What do you want to do with this?" I ask.

She looks at me steadily. "I want the painting. I want what happened to Emil acknowledged. And I want the man who did it to spend the rest of his life understanding that the space between what he did and what could be proven finally closed."

"In that order?"

"In that order," she says.

I put my cup down. "The painting is manageable. The rest—" I look at the letter on the table. "The rest is going to take longer and be harder and involve people you haven't met yet. But it's possible."

"I've been working toward possible for twelve years," Ruth Kleiner says. "Possible suits me."
write the scene where Dace confronts Fenn directly

**Fenn**

Harold Fenn's office is on the fourteenth floor of the building with his name on it, and the view on a clear day would presumably be magnificent. Today the view is rain, moving in sheets across the glass that runs floor to ceiling on the river side, and the city below is a grey arrangement of wet rooftops disappearing into cloud.

His secretary tries twice to stop me. I walk past her the second time, which produces a quality of objection I don't fully hear, and push the office door open.

Fenn is at his desk. Sixty-three and substantial, the kind of man who has been posed for photographs long enough that he arranges his face as a reflex. He looks at me and the arrangement is very good — barely a flicker.

"I don't know you," he says.

"My name is Dace." I close the door. The rain outside the floor-to-ceiling glass turns the city below into a watercolour. "I've been hired by a woman named Ruth Kleiner. Her family owned a painting called The Interior, Afternoon, which is currently hanging in your collection under the provenance of Emil Varga."

The face does something small. Recovers quickly.

"I have no idea what you're—"

"Emil Varga is dead," I say. "He wrote a letter before he died. Ruth has it. I have copies." I sit down across from him without being asked. "I also have a man named Solomon Roth who will testify to what Varga told him. I have the original provenance documents from the Prague collection. And I have a forensic examiner who would be very interested in the drag marks on Emil Varga's scaffold platform."

Fenn is very still. The rain on the glass behind him is very loud.

"You went to considerable trouble," I say, "to have a painter authenticate something you couldn't explain owning. And when he lost his nerve, you went to more considerable trouble. That's the shape of what I have."

"What do you want?" he says. His voice is flat now. The civic warmth is gone and what's underneath is cooler and older.

"I don't want anything." I look at him. "Ruth Kleiner wants the painting. The letter is going to law enforcement and to a journalist who covers art repatriation for a national paper, simultaneously, in two hours. That's already arranged and it's not conditional on this conversation."

He looks at his desk. At his hands. At the rain on the glass.

"You're not here to make a deal," he says.

"No," I say. "I'm here so that you know it's coming and why. Emil Varga deserved someone to look him in the eye when the thing he was afraid of finally arrived. You're going to have to settle for me."

I stand up.

Fenn says nothing. The rain presses against the glass behind him in continuous grey sheets. The city below goes about its wet afternoon, not yet knowing what the afternoon contains.

I leave him with the rain and the view and the weight of a conversation he's been having in one form or another for twenty years.
write the resolution of the painter case — not triumphant, appropriately bittersweet

**Aftermath**

It takes four months.

Fenn's lawyers are good, which I expected, and the process of establishing provenance for a work that has been publicly attributed to another painter for eleven years is complicated enough to keep several people employed for most of those four months. I'm not involved in most of it — my part is done — but Kren keeps me informed because he has no one else to keep informed.

The rain is intermittent through those months but today, when I drive to the gallery where the transfer is happening, it has come back in the dependable way it does in this city, as if it keeps a calendar.

The gallery is on Museum Row and is not Fenn's own collection space — it's a neutral location, which was Ruth's requirement, arranged by the cultural attaché from the consulate who has been coordinating the repatriation paperwork. The formalities are already done when I arrive. I stand in the back of the small room and watch a painting be moved.

The Interior, Afternoon.

It's smaller than I expected and more particular — not a grand work, not an obvious masterpiece, but a painting of a specific room in a specific afternoon light that someone spent a long time learning to see correctly. The kind of painting that exists because someone loved a room and wanted to hold the quality of light in it.

Ruth stands in front of it for a long time. She doesn't touch it. She doesn't cry, at least not while I'm watching — she stands with her hands at her sides and looks at it with the expression of someone reconnecting a severed thing, slowly, carefully, not trusting the reconnection not to break.

Kren is beside her. He looks at the painting with the eye of someone who was trained by the man who spent eleven years pretending it was his.

Fenn is not in custody. That part hasn't resolved and may not — the evidence for what happened to Emil is circumstantial and the DA's office is moving carefully. He has resigned from the arts endowment board and from his hospital committees and his name is still on the building on Fifth but the cleaning schedule for the letters has apparently lapsed.

I don't consider that sufficient. I don't consider it insufficient. I consider it the shape of what was possible, which is usually a different shape than what was deserved.

The rain comes down on Museum Row and on the gallery skylight and on the street where my car is parked and on the building with Fenn's name on it across the river.

Ruth turns and finds me in the back of the room. She comes over and she doesn't say thank you, which I appreciate — she just looks at me for a moment with the expression of someone who is partway through a long process and is acknowledging a particular step.

"What happens now?" I ask.

"I take it home," she says. "I look at it every day. I remember who bought it and why." She pauses. "And I keep looking for the others."

The others. There are lists of others — items from the Prague collection that went in various directions in 1939 and have not come back. It is, I understand from our conversations, a life's work that keeps expanding rather than contracting.

"Good luck," I say.

"Thank you, Mr. Dace." She says it like she means the whole four months, not just the conversation.

I go back out into the rain and get into my car and drive back to my office, where a new envelope is almost certainly not waiting on my desk, and where the window needs the rain to remind it what glass is for, and where a cigarette is waiting that I haven't decided about yet.

I drive in the rain. The city goes past.

Some things come back. Some don't. The work is in learning which is which.
write a scene showing Dace's daily life — between cases, something quiet and character-driven

**Between**

The rain today is the soft continuous kind that isn't making a point, and I sit in Dot's diner on Clement Street at seven in the morning and read the paper and drink coffee that is not as good as the coffee Ruth Kleiner made in Brooklyn and do not think about that particular comparison.

Dot refills my cup without asking. She has been doing this for six years, which is also how long I've been coming here, and our relationship consists almost entirely of this transaction, which suits us both.

The paper has nothing about Carver. Carver has been in a federal facility in Maryland for three months and the case is proceeding with the slowness of cases that involve judges and aldermen and men whose lawyers know every procedural inlet and cove in the legal coastline. Flynn writes about it occasionally. Malone calls occasionally. The city has largely moved on to other arrangements, because that's what cities do.

I think about the Kleiner case the way I think about the Voss case — not with satisfaction exactly, which is too clean a word, but with the specific quality of having done what was asked and a version of what was right and understanding that those two things overlapping is not guaranteed and is worth noting when it happens.

The diner has three other customers this early. A man in a delivery uniform eating eggs. A woman with a briefcase and the expression of someone already composing an argument she'll make in an hour. A kid at the counter who is looking at the rain on the window with the particular attention of someone who is trying not to be wherever he's supposed to be.

I recognise that look.

"Scratch," I say.

He turns around on his stool. The recognition takes a moment — he's seen me once, in the rain, under an awning — and then it arrives and his face does the cat calculation again.

"You're the detective," he says.

"You're a long way from Eleventh," I say.

He shrugs. "Manny says this diner has better coffee."

"Manny is right about most things." I nod at Dot, who appears with another cup without being asked, and sets it in front of the kid. "Are you eating?"

He looks at the cup. At me. At the cup again. "If you're buying."

"I'm buying."

We sit at the counter and the rain comes down on Clement Street and Dot brings eggs without asking because she has identified a situation and has opinions about it, and the kid eats with the focus of someone for whom a meal at a counter is not a routine occurrence.

I don't ask him anything. He doesn't volunteer anything. The rain makes the window beside us a soft moving thing.

After a while he says, unprompted: "The man from the Blue Canary. The bartender. He's not there anymore."

"I heard that," I say. "He's cooperating with a federal investigation."

"And the big boss?"

"In Maryland."

Scratch considers this with the thoughtfulness of someone who doesn't divide the world into good and bad outcomes but into better and worse arrangements. "Does that mean the block is safer?"

I think about it honestly. "For a while," I say. "Until the next arrangement."

He nods. This is apparently a satisfactory answer — more satisfactory, it seems, than if I'd said yes.

Dot refills both our cups. The rain keeps going.
write a scene where a case from Dace's past comes back to haunt him — someone he couldn't help

**An Old Name**

The rain finds the crack in my office window the way it always does at this time of year, laying a thin cold line of damp across the sill that I deal with by putting a towel there and not thinking about it. It's past eight and the office is dark except for my lamp and the shifting grey-white of the rain-wet street below.

The phone rings.

"Is this the detective?" A woman's voice. Young, or sounding young — there's a quality to it that suggests effort, the effortfulness of someone keeping a thing in their chest that wants to come out differently than a question.

"Dace," I say.

"My name is Soline Hecht. You — three years ago you worked for my mother."

I am quiet for a moment. The rain inserts itself into the silence on the sill. I know the name Hecht. I know what three years ago means in the context of that name.

Her mother was a woman named Petra who came to me about a man she was afraid of — not her husband, a man who had been her employer, who had made certain arrangements that she wanted documented and certain threats that she wanted witnesses to. I took the case. I built a file. The file was good. The evening I was going to bring it to Malone, Petra Hecht called me from a payphone in the rain to tell me it was finished, she was handling it herself, she didn't need me anymore.

She was dead forty-eight hours later. The official cause was an accidental fall from a staircase. I know what I know about the difference between that kind of accident and other kinds.

The man she was afraid of sold his business interest in the city and moved to Los Angeles and has not been in my jurisdiction since.

"I know who you are," I say.

"Then you know what happened to her," Soline says. Her voice has steadied. The thing she was keeping in her chest has apparently found its proper form, which is not grief but something cooler. "I'm twenty-three now. I've been watching him from a distance for three years. He's coming back to the city in two weeks. He's negotiating a business arrangement with a property group on the east side."

The rain comes down on my window and on the crack in the sill and on the city below.

"What do you want from me?" I ask.

"I want to know if there's anything left of the file you built," she says.

I look at my filing cabinet. I look at the rain on the window. I think about a woman calling from a payphone, rain in the background of the call, the last conversation we had.

"Yes," I say. "There's a file."

Soline Hecht is quiet for a moment. The rain comes down on whatever city she's calling from. "Then I'd like to come in," she says. "Tomorrow, if that suits."

"Come in," I say. "I'll have the coffee on."

I hang up and sit in the dark of my office with my hand on the phone and the rain finding the crack in the window and the file in the cabinet and the three years of a thing I didn't finish.

Some cases close. Some wait.

This one has been waiting.
write the scene where Soline comes to the office — describe her and how she sees the old file

**Soline**

She arrives at nine on a morning when the rain is the specific grey-white of old newsprint and falls without wind so that it seems to come from directly above, vertical and deliberate.

Soline Hecht is tall and dark-haired and looks enough like her mother to produce in me a specific unpleasant feeling that I keep off my face with practice. She wears a grey coat and carries a leather satchel and she looks around my office the way observant people look at a room they have built a picture of before entering.

I put the file on the desk between us. She looks at it but does not reach for it immediately, which is the right instinct.

"How much do you know about what your mother hired me for?" I ask.

"She did not tell me at the time. I was twenty." She wraps both hands around the coffee cup. "I found her papers after. Not all of them. But enough to understand the shape of it."

"The man is named Conrad Dresser," I say. "He is returning to the city in two weeks. In the file there are records of seven transactions she documented. She was thorough." I pause. "She was more thorough than I had asked her to be, which is part of why she moved faster than I could."

Soline picks up the file. She opens it and reads in the grey-white light of the rain on the window. I drink my coffee and do not say anything, because there is nothing useful to say while someone reads the last careful work of their dead mother.

She closes it after a long time.

"What would it take," she says, "to use this now?"

"Corroboration," I say. "Three years is a long time. But Dresser coming back creates an opportunity."

"An opportunity," she says.

"To catch him making the same arrangements in a city where the evidence against his last arrangements already exists."

Soline looks at the window. The rain makes its patterns on the glass. "You have been thinking about this," she says.

"Since you called."

"Since before I called," she says, and she is correct, and I do not deny it.
write the scene where Dace finds the third intermediary — a man living quietly under a new name

**The Third Man**

The rain follows me to the south side and into a neighbourhood of small houses where it collects in the low spots between the flagstones and makes every front path a shallow river.

The man in the file is listed under the name Caufield. He is now living as a Mr. Pryor at a number on Leander Street, according to contacts I spend two days and a moderate sum locating. He works at a hardware store on Fletcher, where he is known as quiet and reliable and nothing else.

I go to the hardware store first. I stand in the rain outside for a few minutes and watch him through the window. A man in his fifties, thin now where the file photograph shows him heavier, with the general quality of someone who has been trying to become unremarkable for three years and has mostly succeeded.

I knock on his door at five-fifteen.

He opens it and sees me and the colour leaves his face in a specific way that tells me he knows who I am even if he does not know my name.

"Mr. Pryor," I say. "Or Caufield, if you prefer."

The rain is falling on both of us from his shallow overhang. He steps back.

Inside is a clean small room. No photographs. No papers visible. The house of someone who has stripped their life down to what cannot incriminate them.

"I do not have anything to do with Dresser anymore," he says, before I have said a word.

"I know." I sit in the chair he does not offer. "But Dresser is coming back. And when he does, he will try to make contact. I want you to answer when he calls. And I want you to tell me when he does."

Pryor looks at me. "That is all?"

"That is the beginning."

He looks at the window for a long time. The rain makes the glass grey and moving.

"There is a woman named Hecht," he says. "Her daughter."

"Yes."

"I knew what happened to her mother. I was already gone by then. But I knew."

I look at him. The rain comes down. "When Dresser calls, you answer."

Pryor looks at his hands. Then he nods.

"Tell him I am open to the conversation," he says.

"Good," I say, and go back out into the rain.
write the scene where Dresser arrives and makes contact with Dace unexpectedly

**Dresser**

I do not go looking for Conrad Dresser. He finds me, which means he has been watching my office, which means he knows I am connected to Soline Hecht, which means the layers of this case have more awareness of each other than I had hoped.

He is waiting in the chair across from my desk when I come back from lunch on a Wednesday. The rain is steady and moderate on the window and my door was locked, which tells me something about his resources.

He is a large man, well-kept, with the kind of tan that comes from places where the weather is deliberately better than here. He sits in my client chair with the relaxed confidence of someone who is used to being in rooms he does not own.

"Mr. Dace," he says. Conversational. Like we have met.

"You are in my office," I say, from the doorway.

"I apologize for the informality." He gestures at the chair on my side of the desk. "Please."

I come in. I stay standing. The rain on the window gives the room a grey underwater quality. "What do you want?"
