"I want to understand the nature of your interest in my return to the city. I have sources who tell me a private detective has been asking questions about my upcoming business arrangements."

"That is very civic-minded," I say.

"I am a civic-minded person." He looks at me pleasantly. "A woman named Soline Hecht came to your office recently."

"Lots of people come to my office."

"Her mother had documents she should not have had. I prefer to understand whether those documents are still in circulation."

The rain comes down. I look at him and think about Petra Hecht calling from a payphone three years ago and what the forty-eight hours after that call looked like.

"I would not know anything about that," I say.

Dresser studies me. He has the eyes of someone who is very good at determining when they are being told the truth. He stands.

"I hope," he says pleasantly, "that we can maintain a productive mutual understanding."

"I will give it some thought," I say.

He goes to the door and pauses. "Soline Hecht is a young woman. Young people sometimes pursue things that are not good for them."

He closes the door. The rain comes down on my window and I think about how fast I need to move and in which order.

Faster than yesterday. The order has just changed.
write the tense scene where Dace gets Soline somewhere safe — Dresser is watching her

**Movement**

I call Soline and the phone rings six times and I am on the seventh ring calculating how fast I can reach her address when she picks up.

"Do not go outside," I say.

She is quiet for exactly one second. "What happened?"

"Dresser came to my office. He knows you have been to see me. He is watching you." I am already in my coat. "I am coming to you. Do not open the door for anyone until I knock three times."

"Understood," she says, and hangs up.

The rain outside is the heaviest it has been in a week. I run two blocks to my car through it and I am soaked before I am in the driver seat. I drive to Croydon via Arch Street, which loops away from the direction a watch on my office would expect me to go.

I knock three times.

Soline opens the door with her coat already on and her satchel on her shoulder. She used the time I was driving to prepare rather than to panic, which is the right use of the time.

We go out the back through the service alley. The rain in the alley is hard and loud on the metal fire escapes above us and the puddles underfoot are ankle-deep in the low spots.

"Where are we going?" she says.

"Roth," I say. Old Roth is on the seventh floor of the Belvedere and opens his door to people in trouble without requiring explanation, and Dresser has no reason to know about him yet.

We reach the car. We get in. The rain hits the roof in sustained waves.

"He threatened you," she says.

"He made a remark about young people and things that are not good for them."

Soline looks out the rain-streaked window. "He said something similar to my mother once. She told me about it. Exactly once."

I drive. The rain makes the streets into rivers and the streetlights into smears.

"Pryor called this morning," I say. "Dresser reached out. Tonight, apparently."

"That is fast."

"He is making his arrangements before he thinks I have had time to make mine." I glance at her. "He is wrong about the timing."

Soline is quiet. "What is the plan?"

"Pryor wears a wire to the meeting. Malone on the other end. What Dresser says tonight, in the context of your mother's file, is the corroboration I have been missing."

"And if it does not go cleanly?"

"Then we try the next thing," I say. "There is always a next thing."

The rain comes down. The Belvedere appears out of the grey ahead of us.
write the wire scene — Pryor meets Dresser, Dace and Malone listen in the rain

**The Wire**

Malone and I sit in his car on Leander Street with a receiver between us and the rain on the windows turning the street outside into a blur, and we listen.

Pryor meets Dresser at a bar two blocks away. I hear the ambient noise of it, the bar sounds, the rain outside when the door opens and closes. Pryor's voice is steady. Dresser's voice is smooth. They talk for twenty minutes about the old business and the new arrangements and the specific favour Dresser needs, which is Pryor's name on documents and his appearance at two meetings, nothing more than that, he says, as though nothing more than that is a small thing.

Malone makes notes. His face does the thing it does when something confirms what he already knows and he does not find it satisfying so much as necessary.

Then Pryor says: there is a detective. Dace. He came to my house.

A pause on the recording.

I know, Dresser says.

He asked about Petra Hecht.

The bar sounds continue. The rain on the receiver makes a soft grey static. Malone and I both stop breathing.

Petra Hecht, Dresser says, had documents she should not have had and a habit of involving herself in things that were none of her business. It is unfortunate what happened. A pause. Unfortunate accidents happen to people who do not understand the value of staying in their lane.

Malone looks at me.

Is that a fact, Pryor says on the recording, and his voice is careful and steady, and I understand that whatever he has been carrying for three years, he has just put it down.

It is just a fact, Dresser says. Are we going to have a problem?

No problem, Pryor says.

The conversation moves back to the arrangements. Malone writes steadily. The rain on the car roof is the specific sound of patience rewarded, which is the best sound I know.

"That is it," Malone says, when the wire goes quiet.

"Is that enough?" I ask.

He clicks the recorder off. He looks at his notes. He looks at the rain on the windshield.

"Combined with the file from three years ago and Pryor's testimony and whatever I can pull on his current arrangements," he says, "yes. That is enough."

The rain comes down on Leander Street and on the bar two blocks away where Pryor sits with whatever he feels and on a city that has been making room for men like Dresser for a very long time.

"Good," I say.
write the scene where Dace tells Soline the Hecht case is resolved

**Telling Her**

I go back to the Belvedere in the rain, which has softened since the wire session into the ambient sound of a wet city breathing.

Roth answers when I knock and I find Soline in his sitting room surrounded by his art, sitting in the chair nearest the window with a cup of something and the expression of someone who has been very still for several hours.

I sit across from her. Roth retreats to his study with the tact of a man who understands the shape of a private conversation.

"Dresser is in custody," I say. "Malone made the arrest two hours ago. The recording combined with your mother's file and Pryor's testimony is enough to hold him tonight and pursue the rest as the investigation develops."

Soline looks at the window. The rain makes slow lines on the glass.

"And the rest," she says. "My mother."

"The recording has him making a statement. Not a confession. He is careful. But a statement about an accident that is inconsistent with the official account and consistent with what we know. Malone says it is useful. Whether it becomes a charge—" I pause. "I cannot promise you what it becomes. I can tell you it is in the record now. Officially. In the right hands."

She is quiet for a long time. The rain runs down the glass and the paintings on Roth's walls absorb what light there is.

"Is that enough?" she asks.

"I do not know," I say, because it is the honest answer. "It is what I have. It is the best version of what is possible."

Soline looks at the window. At the rain. At her hands around the cup.

"She would say that is something," she says finally. "She was practical about what is possible." A pause. "I am less practical."

"That is not a flaw," I say.

She looks at me. Something in her face settles.

"Thank you, Mr. Dace," she says.

I nod once and stand and go back out into the evening rain, which is soft now and falling straight and does not need to make a point anymore, and I walk the five blocks to my car in the specific silence of a case that has done what cases can do.
write a scene showing Dace's relationship with Malone more deeply — they meet for a drink

**Malone, Off the Clock**

We meet at a bar on Gower Street where the rain hammers the awning with real commitment and the sound inside is the specific ambient roar of rain doing its best work against an old roof. Malone has a whiskey and I have the same and we sit in a booth near the back and do not talk for a while, which is how we start most non-professional conversations.

"Fourteen years," Malone says eventually.

"In the department?"

"Since I decided not to do what they were offering me. The arrangement." He looks at his glass. "A lieutenant would be useful to certain people in this city. I have been offered the promotion four times."

"You have said no four times."

"Yes." He drinks. "My ex-wife says I am punishing myself. My priest says I am being stubborn. I think they are both right and neither one has the full picture."

I look at the rain on the bar's front window. "What is the full picture?"

"The full picture is that I do not know how to do a different job. I know how to be a detective in a complicated city. I do not know how to be a lieutenant in the same city, because the things a lieutenant has to manage would require me to look away from certain things, and I do not know how to look away."

The rain comes down on the old roof with sustained enthusiasm.

"Is that a choice or an inability?" I ask.

"Both," he says, "the same way most things are." He looks at me. "You have been doing this for what, eight years?"

"Eleven."

"Do you know how to do a different job?"

I think about it honestly. "I know how to think about things until they become clear. I am not sure that qualifies as a job in any other context."

Malone almost smiles. It is a very small almost.

"To impractical people," he says, and raises his glass.

"To impractical people," I say, and we drink, and the rain comes down on Gower Street with democratic persistence.
write the opening of a third case — lighter in tone, almost comedic

**Case Three: The Dog**

The rain on my window is the light morning variety and my coffee is still hot and I have a newspaper and no appointments and I am in the middle of a moment of something close to contentment when Mrs. Harriet Booth arrives.

She is sixty, substantial, and wearing a hat that has not been deterred by the rain. She sits across from my desk with the upright quality of a woman who has opinions about posture and has never revised them.

"My dog," she says, "has been stolen."

I look at her. "Have you gone to the police?"

"The police," she says, with a quality in her voice that removes the police from further consideration, "told me to file a report. I filed a report. Do I look like a woman who came here for a filed report?"

I look at the rain on my window. I think about the Carver case. I think about Petra Hecht and Soline and a warehouse on the waterfront. I think about whether I have the moral energy for something smaller.

"What kind of dog?" I ask.

"His name is Winston and he is a dachshund of considerable distinction," Mrs. Booth says. "He has won three shows and is entered in a fourth next month and if he is not returned to me by then I will not be responsible for what I do next." She opens her purse. "His estimated value—"

"I am sure it is substantial," I say. "When did he go missing?"

"Yesterday morning. I left him with my neighbour, Mr. Abramowitz, while I went to my appointment. When I returned, Mr. Abramowitz said Winston had simply gone out through the garden gate, which was inexplicably open."

"Inexplicably," I say.

"Mr. Abramowitz has many fine qualities," Mrs. Booth says, with the delivery of someone who has curated this list carefully and found it shorter than expected. "Closing gates is not among them."

The rain comes down lightly on my window. I put down my newspaper.

"Tell me about Winston," I say, and reach for my pad.
write the scene where the dog case takes an unexpected turn — not as simple as it seemed

**Winston's Complicated Life**

I find Winston inside of four hours, which is almost the fastest I have found anything in eleven years of professional finding. He is in an apartment on the third floor of a building on Marsden, in the care of a young woman named Cecily Abramowitz who is the niece of the neighbour Mrs. Booth described as gate-inept.

Cecily is twenty-two and Winston is sitting in her lap and they are both, when I knock on the door and explain the situation, united in the specific unhappiness of a reunion being proposed that neither of them wants.

"He is Mrs. Booth's dog," I say.

"He was," Cecily says. The rain outside her window is light and she has a cup of tea. Winston is wearing a knitted sweater that I suspect is not his original property. "She bought him from my uncle four years ago. My uncle bred him. The agreement was that she would have him for shows and my uncle could keep breeding rights. She has not honoured that agreement in two years and she is entering him in a show next month that my uncle specifically asked her not to enter him in because he is too old for the circuit now and it is bad for him."

I look at Winston. Winston has the expression of a dog who has a detailed perspective on his own situation and is displeased that no one has thought to ask.

"So your uncle opened the gate," I say.

"And I happened to be visiting," Cecily says, pleasantly.

I sit down, because this is the kind of case that requires sitting. The rain patters on her window and Winston sighs the sigh of a senior dachshund who has seen too much.

"There are two sides to this," I say.

"There are three sides," Cecily says. "Winston's is the most important one."

I look at the dog. The dog looks at me. The rain comes down.

"I am going to need to make some calls," I say.
write the resolution of the dog case — a surprisingly satisfying ending

**Resolution, Winston**

I spend two days in the rain going between Mrs. Booth, Mr. Abramowitz, and Cecily, which is not the most consequential work I have done in eleven years but is, I discover, more complicated than some murder cases I have read about.

The solution I arrive at is this: Winston is retired from the show circuit, which the veterinarian confirms is medically advisable. Mrs. Booth retains ownership, which is legally correct. Cecily Abramowitz takes Winston on Tuesdays and Thursdays, which he is informed of via the mechanism of simply bringing him to her apartment on those days and observing that he demonstrates no objection. Mr. Abramowitz installs a proper latch on the garden gate in the rain on a Wednesday afternoon with the thoroughness of a man who has been waiting for a specific excuse.

I present the arrangement to all parties on a Thursday morning in Mrs. Booth's sitting room, where the rain on the windows is the quiet morning variety and Winston is already on Cecily's lap.

Mrs. Booth looks at Winston in Cecily's lap for a long moment.

"He does look comfortable," she says, with the quality of someone revising a position that has cost her something.

"He is a social dog," Cecily says, carefully.

"Yes," Mrs. Booth says. "He always was." A pause. "His mother was the same."

This is, I understand, as much as Mrs. Booth is going to give, and it is more than I expected, and Cecily understands this too and does not push further.

Mrs. Booth pays me. She is precise about the amount.

At the door she pauses. "You are not what I expected from a detective."

"What did you expect?"

"Someone who would simply return the dog," she says.

"Would that have solved it?" I ask.

She considers. "No," she says. "It would not." And she closes the door.

I stand on her front step in the light rain and look at the wet street and think about a dachshund in a knitted sweater who got what he needed from a situation by simply being himself, which is more than most of my clients manage.

I put my collar up and walk back through the rain to wherever the next thing is.
write a reflective scene — Dace thinks honestly about why he does this work

**Why**

The rain tonight is the long-haul kind and I sit in my office at ten with the lamp on and no case file open and ask myself the question I ask periodically, which is: why.

Not in crisis. Not in despair. Just in the honest accounting that late evenings in rain produce in me.

The answer I always arrive at first is the wrong one: I do this because I am good at it, which is true but explains nothing about why anyone does anything.

The second answer is closer: I do this because the problems have solutions. Not always. Not cleanly. But the problems I take have, at their centre, a fact. Something that happened. Something that was taken. Someone who did something. And facts can be found. This is different from most of what I observe people spending their lives on, which are problems that do not have facts at their centre, only feelings and arrangements and the accumulated weight of choices no one is accountable for.

I find the facts. That is the job. Someone put Martin Voss in a car and the fact of it was real and findable. Emil Varga was moved after his death and the marks on the scaffold boards were real and findable. Petra Hecht knew something and was killed for it and the man who arranged it said something into a wire in a bar in the rain, and now that is a fact in a file in Malone's hands.

I do not always get them into the right hands in time. I did not, with Petra. I do not always get results that match the fact's weight. Fenn still has a building with his name on it. Some things that should close do not close.

But facts do not un-become facts. That is what I sit with tonight, in the rain, with the lamp on.

I find them. I put them in the record. What the record does with them is someone else's job, and I have made my peace with that division of labour, more or less, on most nights.

Tonight is a more-or-less night.

I turn off the lamp and sit with the rain on the window and that is enough.
write a scene where Dace initially refuses a case but then takes it anyway

**The Refusal and After**

The man who comes to my office on a Tuesday in the rain is named Pellegrini, and I know the name because Malone has mentioned it in the category of names adjacent to investigations without being in them.

He sits across from me with a precise and expensive manner and tells me what he wants, which is surveillance on his business partner.

I tell him no.

He is surprised. His kind usually are. They arrive at a rate and expect everything at that rate, and no is not a word they encounter often in rooms they have decided to enter.

"I know your business partner," I say. "We have met. Not as a client. As a person who helped me with a case two years ago in a way he was not obligated to. I do not take cases against people I have had dealings with."

"That is—" he starts.

"That is my policy," I say. "You are welcome to find someone who does not have it."

He leaves in the rain, which is falling at a particular slant I find appropriate to the situation.

I sit with my refusal for half an hour. I feel good about it. I drink my coffee. I read three paragraphs of the paper.

Then I think about what Pellegrini specifically said. That he suspects financial infidelity from a partner who controls the company's import accounts. And I think about a man named Dresser and a city that moves things through import accounts and I think about whether the partner Pellegrini is surveilling knows something Pellegrini does not want him to know.

I call Malone.

"Pellegrini," I say. "What is his connection to the import business on the east side?"

Malone is quiet for a moment. "Why are you asking?"

"Because he just came to my office and wanted surveillance on his partner."

"And?"

"And I would like to know which one of them I should be more interested in."

Malone tells me something that changes the shape of the afternoon.

I put on my coat and go back out into the rain with the specific purpose of finding the partner before Pellegrini does.
write a scene where the kid Scratch appears again — older, surprising Dace

**Scratch, Two Years Later**

I see him outside the courthouse on a Wednesday, standing in the rain under the wide overhang with a briefcase and a dark coat that fits him correctly.

It takes me a moment because the last time I saw him he was fourteen and eating eggs at Dot's counter. He is now, by my calculation, sixteen. But he stands like someone has been teaching him things, and the briefcase is real and has been used, and he watches me recognise him with the specific patience of someone who has thought about how this moment would go.

"Scratch," I say.

"Nobody calls me that anymore," he says. "It is Nico."

"Nico." I look at the courthouse behind him. At the briefcase. "What are you doing here?"

"Delivering documents for Mr. Ellworth's firm. I have been working for him six months."

Ellworth is a defense attorney of the serious kind. The rain comes down on the courthouse steps and on the overhang and on the city behind us, and I look at Scratch, who is sixteen and working in a law firm and standing under a courthouse overhang with appropriate footwear.

"How did that happen?" I ask.

"You," he says, simply. "You gave me Malone's name after the Carver thing. Malone talked to a social worker. The social worker talked to a program. The program talked to Mr. Ellworth who always takes one person from the program." He holds the briefcase. "I am good at details. It turns out lawyers need people who are good at details."

I think about a kid under an awning in the rain who caught two coins without looking and described a car in the dark with accuracy and stayed behind trash cans when a dangerous man looked up.

"Yes," I say. "They do."

The rain comes down. He has somewhere to be and he knows it and he goes, with the particular quality of someone who has acquired a schedule and respects it.

I stand under the overhang for a moment in the rain, alone, and think about the specific ways this city sometimes surprises you.
write the final scene — Dace in his office, end of another day, looking out at the rain. a perfect ending

**The Office, Again**

The rain comes back in the evening the way it always does.

I sit in my office with the lamp on and the window cracked an inch and the city below going about its evening in the wet. I have a cigarette tonight that I actually smoke, because some evenings are cigarette evenings and I have stopped arguing with the distinction.

On my desk: the Pellegrini file, which has become something else entirely and is going to take another three weeks and will resolve badly for Pellegrini and correctly for the city, which is the usual arrangement. Soline Hecht sends me a note occasionally. She is in law school, which I find improbable and completely correct. Ruth Kleiner sent me a card at Christmas from Brooklyn with a photograph of The Interior, Afternoon hanging in her front room, which is a small room and not a gallery and is the right wall for it.

Malone is still a detective. He has been offered the promotion a fifth time and said no a fifth time.

Nico passes the courthouse most Wednesdays. I have seen him twice more since the first time. He nods. I nod. This is sufficient.

I smoke the cigarette and look at the rain on my window.

The city below is the city it has always been: arranged, compromised, occasionally magnificent, continuously itself. It makes its accommodations and some of those accommodations produce men like Carver and Dresser and Fenn, and some of those men are stopped and some move on to arrangements in other cities and the city rearranges itself around what remains.

I find the facts. I put them in the record. I let the record do what records do.

The rain picks up outside. My window holds it.

Somewhere in this city a woman sits with a painting that belongs to her. Somewhere a man who helped me with a warehouse on the waterfront is eating dinner with his wife. Somewhere a sixteen-year-old who knows how to catch coins without looking is learning what lawyers need.

I put the cigarette out.

I close the window against the rain.

It keeps falling anyway.

Outside, the city is wet and complicated and ongoing.

I am in it.

That is the job.

**The Rain's Account**

I fall on the window of a man's office and on the street below and on the courthouse four blocks east and on the diner where a boy with a briefcase has just come in out of me, and on the building on Fifth where a name in lit letters is now sometimes dark because the maintenance has lapsed, and on a house in Brooklyn where a painting hangs on a wall facing the right direction, and on a bar on Gower Street where a detective and a policeman drink whiskey without explaining themselves to each other, and on a warehouse at the waterfront that is now legitimately leased, and on the whole continuing city that turns me into rivers and channels me into the drains and builds its arrangements and its accommodations inside the spaces I leave between one thing and another. I fall on a man's closed window and I keep falling after he closes it, because I was here before the window and I will be here after, and in the meantime the city and I have our arrangement: I come, it absorbs, we go on. The man in the office is part of this the way everything in the city is part of this. He finds facts. I find everything else. Between us, most of what matters gets found.
write a short scene — Dace gets a late night call that turns out to be nothing. the texture of the work

**Nothing**

The phone rings at eleven forty-five and I am in my chair with my shoes off and the rain on the window doing its late-night thing, which is quieter and more personal than the daytime rain.

I say my name.

Breathing. Then: I think someone is following me.

A man's voice. Older, cautious. I sit up.

"Tell me what you are seeing," I say.

He tells me: a dark car, parked across from his house two nights in a row. A man at the end of the street this morning who looked away when he looked. A feeling he has had for a week that he cannot point at specifically.

I ask him three questions. His name, his address, and whether he has any reason he can name for why someone would be watching him.

His name is Gerald Pryce. His address is on Elm. His reason, when he gives it: he testified in a civil case last spring, a dispute between neighbours, nothing significant.

The rain comes down on my window and I think about Carver and Dresser and the genuine texture of being watched, which is a different thing from Gerald Pryce's feeling.

"Mr. Pryce," I say. "The dark car. What make?"

He describes it. A common model in a common colour.

"Is your neighbour on the other side of you also named Pryce?"

A pause. "My brother. He visits on Wednesdays."

"Does he drive a dark green sedan?"

A longer pause.

Oh, Gerald Pryce says.

"The man at the end of the street. Was he walking a dog?"

The longest pause. He was, actually.

"Mr. Pryce, I think you have been having a difficult week and I think your brother has been visiting and your neighbour has a dog."

A silence. Then, very quietly: I am sorry to have bothered you.

"Do not be," I say. "Call me if the car comes back on a day your brother is out of town."

I put the phone down and sit in the late rain and feel the specific satisfaction of a thing that turned out to be nothing, which is, when you think about it, the best possible outcome.

I put my shoes back on anyway. It is a habit.
write a scene where Dace runs into Elena Voss by chance — a brief, complicated encounter

**By Chance**

I see her outside the Museum of the City on a Saturday, which is the kind of coincidence that a city this size should not produce and keeps producing anyway.

The rain is light, the kind of Saturday rain that is not quite committing, stopping and starting.

She is coming down the museum steps with Martin, who looks better than the last time I saw him. They have a child with them. A small girl, perhaps three, wearing rain boots that are considerably too large and navigating the steps with the concentration of a mountaineer.

Elena sees me first. She stops on the second-to-last step.

I stop on the pavement.

Martin looks up and sees me and the quality of his expression is the complicated kind. Gratitude and something less comfortable than gratitude, because the person who pulled you out of a warehouse is a person who knows you were in the warehouse, and that knowledge has a weight.

"Mr. Dace," Elena says.

"Mrs. Voss," I say.

A moment in the light rain. The child on the steps says something to her boots and they negotiate the last step together.

"This is Petra," Elena says, looking at the child. Then she looks at me, and I understand that it is not a coincidence of naming.

I look at the girl, who looks at me with the comprehensive assessment of a three-year-old and then loses interest in favour of a puddle.

"That is a good name," I say.

"Yes," Elena says.

Martin puts his hand briefly on my arm, the gesture of a man who does not have the right words and knows it and uses what he has. I nod once.

We go our separate ways. The rain comes back, light and indecisive, as the city moves around us.

I walk two blocks before I put the encounter in whatever file I keep for things that are complete and still present, which is not a formal file but is one of the more used ones I maintain.
write a late-career scene — Dace considers retirement, then gets a call that changes his mind

**The Question of Retirement**

I am fifty-three and it is raining, which is a sentence that could describe any number of evenings but describes this one specifically.

I sit in Dot's diner with my coffee going cold beside the newspaper and I think about the word retirement, which Malone used two weeks ago in a conversation ostensibly about a pension arrangement but actually about something else. He is fifty-eight and is having the conversation with himself that people in this kind of work eventually have, and he has started having it out loud with me, which means I am also now having it.

I look at my reflection in the diner window. The rain makes it soft and moveable. I think about what retirement means in practice: no more files, no more rain-soaked stakeouts, no more men in my office chair when I return from lunch. A kind of stillness.

I am trying to determine whether I want stillness.

Dot refills my coffee without asking, which she has been doing for twelve years. I think about twelve years of the same refill, the same counter, the same window with the rain on it.

Then the diner phone rings. Dot answers. She looks at me.

"For you," she says.

I take the receiver.

The voice on the other end is Malone's. "I have something," he says. "A case that came in this morning. It is the kind you should hear about."

"Why me?"

"Because the woman who brought it in said she was told to ask for a private detective named Dace. By a man in a federal facility in Maryland named Carver, who has apparently decided to cooperate with something in exchange for something and considers this particular information your professional territory."

The rain comes down on the diner window. Dot watches me from a careful distance.

"I will come to the office," I say.

I put the receiver down. I drink the coffee Dot has kept hot. I put on my coat.

The question of retirement has resolved itself in the specific way that questions resolve when the answer was always obvious and only needed the right phone call to become visible.

I push through the diner door into the rain.
write the very last scene — Dace older, walking through the rain one last time

**The Last Walk**

I am sixty-one and the rain is the autumn kind, cold enough to feel and not cold enough to be called anything else, and I walk from my office to Dot's diner on Clement Street in it without hurrying.

I passed the case to Nico six months ago. Not formally. In the way of a person who has shown someone everything they know and watched them do it better. He is twenty-seven and has three years of law behind him and the particular quality of attention that makes a good investigator, which is not cleverness exactly but the willingness to look at something until it becomes clear.

I still consult. Malone calls occasionally. Flynn calls when something needs a specific kind of reading. The work has changed shape rather than ended, which is the retirement I was always going to accept.

I walk through the rain on Clement Street and pass the newsstand where Manny has been for longer than I have been coming to this neighbourhood. I pass the building where the Blue Canary used to be, which is now a different bar with a better reputation and worse music. I pass the courthouse where a boy I know carries briefcases and will eventually argue cases.

The city moves around me in the rain. It makes its arrangements. Some of those arrangements are wrong and will need to be found and put in the record by someone patient enough to look. That someone is now usually Nico. Occasionally it is still me.

I push through Dot's door. The rain-sound stops and the diner-sound starts and Dot looks up and the coffee is already being poured before I reach my stool.

I sit down.

I look at the rain on the window.

It falls on Clement Street and on the courthouse and on the Belvedere and on a building on Fifth where the letters are imperfectly lit and on a warehouse at the waterfront with a legitimate lease and on a house in Brooklyn where a painting hangs on the right wall.

It falls on all of it equally, which is the rain's great honesty.

I wrap both hands around the cup.

Outside, the city is ongoing.

Inside, the coffee is hot.

This is enough.
write a one-paragraph author's note on writing in this style and genre

**A Note on the Rain**

The present tense in noir is not a stylistic choice so much as an argument: the detective is always arriving, never having arrived, always in the middle of a thing that is not yet resolved. Past tense creates distance and reflection; present tense creates company. When I write I walk instead of I walked, I put the reader in the same wet street as Dace, making the same calculations, without the protection of retrospect. The rain is related to this. Rain in noir is structural. It functions as the city's continuous commentary, the indifferent witness to everything that happens in its streets. It equalizes. It falls on the corrupt and the honest and the dead and the living and the dachshund in the knitted sweater with identical indifference, and this indifference is not nihilism but its opposite: the insistence that the world continues regardless of what any single person does in it, and that doing the right thing anyway, within the limits of what one person can do, is precisely the kind of work that matters because it does not depend on the rain caring whether it does.
